body_txt: You know that girl: the one who spends her time looking down her nose at the nerdy girl, stealing your crush, humiliating you in front of the whole class, etc. She's the Alpha Bitch , sitting very comfortably (and fabulously) on her throne, at the top of the school social hierarchy… who just got upstaged by the new girl? With the Alpha Bitch 's downfall, her successor, usually a Naïve Newcomer or New Transfer Student in High School (or an equivalent setting), usurps her social throne and becomes the new queen of the school. Whether she was taken under her predecessor's wing and remolded into the image of a popular girl, or if she previously went against the Alpha Bitch out in the open, the usurper has shaken up the status quo. How the usurper succeeds in overthrowing the Alpha Bitch may also vary — it could be via the Insidious Rumor Mill , Passive-Aggressive Kombat , or even a physical fight, especially if she was once a member of the Girl Posse . Alternatively, she could have done so with The Power of Friendship , having had help from others to dethrone the original queen bee, normally from the "losers" of the social hierarchy or even the Girl Posse themselves. If the Alpha Bitch is the more important character, her successor is normally seen as a "knockoff" who "stole" the Alpha Bitch 's "rightful" position at the top of the school hierarchy. If the usurper is the protagonist, she is seen as the feisty cool newcomer who manages to give the Alpha Bitch a taste of her own medicine. After taking the throne, the usurper has two paths: This trope reflects the Popularity Cycle , where the initially popular Alpha Bitch becomes unpopular, while her successor — who usually starts out unpopular, or at least less popular than the original Alpha Bitch — becomes popular instead. Sister Trope of Backstabbing the Alpha Bitch , where a usually more established member of her own Girl Posse , usually the Beta Bitch , challenges her rule and takes over from her; membership of the Girl Posse is not a requirement for this trope, though it can often co-occur. In Japanese media, this will often overlap with The Ojou , with more antagonistic versions engaging in a Noblewoman's Laugh .
